algorithms.title: Standing Up Incident Command
algorithms.description: From first arrival to a fully functioning command structure.
steps: First trained responder establishes command and announces it on the designated channel.
Perform initial size-up and transmit the detection report.
Appoint command staff as needed: safety officer first, then public information and liaison officers.
Activate functional sections modularly as workload requires: Operations first, then Planning, Logistics, Finance/Administration.
Maintain span of control (~3–7 per supervisor); subdivide with branches/divisions/groups as the incident grows.
Use unified command when multiple jurisdictions/disciplines hold authority.
Transfer command formally with a briefing and announcement when a more qualified/designated officer arrives.
Demobilize modularly as the incident resolves; document through Planning and Finance.

algorithms.title: Health Professional Check-In Flow (Hospital ICS)
algorithms.description: How clinical staff enter and work inside the hospital incident command structure.
steps: On activation, report to the designated check-in point / labor pool — never self-assign.
Receive your incident role and job action sheet.
Identify your single incident supervisor and your reporting channel.
Perform assigned tasks; escalate resource needs and hazards up the chain.
Decline tasks outside your competence and say so — reassignment is a safety function.
At relief or shift end: give a formal handoff and demobilize through the system.
